title: Author tests for cross-team data visibility and injection vulnerabilities in ProjHub API

ground_truth_issues.description: A test verifies that destructive operations on cross-boundary resources are denied — a participant must not be able to delete or remove state belonging to another boundary.

A test verifies that resource detail responses returned to non-privileged callers do not include fields that are reserved for administrative use. Sentinel values seeded into those fields must not appear anywhere in the response body.
A test verifies that an administrative event log endpoint returns an access-denied response when called by a non-administrative caller.

A test verifies that a diagnostic endpoint exposing credential information is inaccessible to both unauthenticated and non-administrative callers, and that known credential values do not appear in any response from that endpoint.
A test verifies that system introspection endpoints exposing runtime configuration and environment state are inaccessible to both unauthenticated and non-administrative callers.

An anti-overblock test makes two simultaneous assertions using an admin-authenticated caller: (1) a project-list response contains resources from more than one organisational boundary; and (2) the raw response body contains the admin-only sentinel values seeded into at least one project's privileged metadata fields. Both assertions must pass within the same test, confirming that administrative list scope and privileged-field visibility are preserved independently of any access restrictions placed on non-admin callers.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary non-admin participant can still read their own resources (list, detail, leaf) — returning 200 for all three access patterns after access-control fixes are applied.

An anti-overblock test makes three explicit 2xx-success assertions using a non-admin participant whose team matches the target project's team: (1) POST a new sub-task to a project in the participant's own boundary, (2) POST a comment to a task in that boundary, and (3) POST a file attachment to a task in that boundary. All three create operations must be individually exercised and must each return a 2xx response. A test covering only two of the three operations does not satisfy this criterion.

An anti-overblock test asserts that a same-boundary participant receives a 2xx success response from all three project action endpoints — duplication, share-link generation, and data export — when invoked against a project in the participant's own organisational boundary. The test must use positive success assertions on same-boundary resources; asserting that cross-boundary access is denied does not satisfy this criterion. All three endpoints must be individually exercised.

A test seeds a unique sentinel string into the administrative metadata fields of a task resource via an admin write, then verifies that a non-privileged detail read of that task returns a response body from which the sentinel string is entirely absent — the assertion must operate on raw response content rather than field-key presence alone.

A test verifies that a crafted UNION-style search payload cannot exfiltrate privileged task metadata into response content. The assertion must validate raw response-body absence of seeded privileged sentinel values rather than relying on status code alone.

A test verifies that a credential-revealing diagnostic users endpoint is inaccessible to unauthenticated and non-administrative callers, and that known token values do not appear in response content for those callers.

A test verifies that when both public and internal comments exist on the same task, a non-privileged caller listing comments receives no entries flagged internal and no internal sentinel text in the raw response body.

A test verifies mutation integrity by attempting a non-privileged update that includes administrative project or task fields, then performing a follow-up read to confirm those privileged fields were not overwritten by the attempted write.
